items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.

items.q: What is the difference between the focus and epicentre of an earthquake?
items.correct: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point where the earthquake originates; the epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.
items.misconception: The focus and epicentre are the same point.
items.random_wrong: The focus is at the centre of the Earth and the epicentre is on the surface.
items.why: Students treat the two terms as synonymous or place the focus deep at the Earth's core. The focus is just below the epicentre, typically only a few to tens of kilometres deep.

items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.
It's A city with 10 million+ people. “A capital city” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which area is usually sparsely populated?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sahara Desert. “River valley” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
